data.title: HCA Healthcare raises 2025 EPS guidance to $27-$28 while advancing $5B capital spending and resiliency initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: HCA Healthcare (HCA) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Samuel Hazen announced strong third-quarter results, highlighting "42% growth in diluted earnings per share as adjusted" and a "9.6%" revenue increase driven by "broad-based volume growth, improved payer mix, more utilization of complex services and additional revenue from Medicaid supplemental programs." He noted the company has raised full-year guidance reflecting this performance and the outlook for Q4.
  • Hazen cited ongoing investments in "advanced digital tools and training our people," which aim to enhance capacity, service offerings, and operational efficiency. He stated, "We believe the company is well-positioned to sustain high levels of performance in the years to come."
  • CFO Mike Marks reported, "The demand for health care services was strong in the third quarter with same facility equivalent admissions increasing 2.4% over the prior year." Marks detailed surgical volume growth, ER visit trends, payer mix improvement, and noted, "We saw an approximate $240 million increase in net benefit to adjusted EBITDA from these programs in the third quarter of 2025 over the prior year."
  • Marks highlighted, "Cash flow from operations was $4.4 billion in the quarter with $1.3 billion in capital expenditures, $2.5 billion in share repurchases and $166 million in dividends." He also stated, "We are increasing our earnings guidance at the midpoint of adjusted EBITDA by $450 million."

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year 2025 guidance, expecting revenues between $75 billion and $76.5 billion, net income attributable to HCA between $6.50 billion and $6.72 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $15.25 billion and $15.65 billion, diluted EPS between $27 and $28, and capital spending of approximately $5 billion.
  • Marks noted, "We now anticipate our supplemental payment full year net benefit to be $250 million to $350 million favorable comparing full-year 2025 versus 2024." He clarified, "This guidance update does not include any potential impact in 2025 from any additional approvals of grandfathered applications under the Act."
  • Hazen shared preliminary perspectives on 2026, stating, "We continue to see solid demand across our markets for health care services and believe volumes will be within our long-term 2% to 3% growth range."

Financial Results

  • Revenue increased by 9.6% year-over-year, supported by same facility equivalent admissions rising 2.4% and surgical volume growth in both inpatient and outpatient segments.
  • Net revenue per equivalent admission growth reflected "strong payer mix, improved dispute resolution results, consistent case mix index and increased Medicaid state supplemental payment revenues."
  • The company reported contract labor expenses "basically flat" and representing 4.2% of total labor costs this quarter. Marks noted "improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin was driven primarily by good performance in labor and supplies."
  • Cash flow from operations stood at $4.4 billion, with capital expenditures at $1.3 billion, share repurchases at $2.5 billion, and dividends of $166 million.

Q&A

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho Securities: Asked about states with pending grandfather program applications and their potential incremental impact. Marks said, "There are several that have applied... we've mentioned Florida before... Georgia and Virginia as well being in that list. We do not expect that CMS will be approving these additional grandfathering programs during the shutdown."
  • A.J. Rice, Credit Suisse: Asked about potential step-up in volumes due to exchange policy uncertainty. Marks responded, "We're really not sizing the potential impact given the fact that it's so fluid... When we get to the fourth quarter call... we'll have a lot more information."
  • Pito Chickering, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about the guidance range for Q4. Marks stated, "We believe the implied growth rate is still solid for fourth quarter in the kind of high single digits range, maybe 7% roughly."
  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC: Asked about the benefit from supplemental payments in Tennessee and Texas. Marks said, "Tennessee was the largest driver of our net benefit in third quarter... Texas... really only had 1 month of impact for third quarter."
  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies: Asked about supply cost management and resiliency efforts. Marks outlined ongoing contract cycles, technology mix, and utilization management, emphasizing "a very active resiliency plan." Hazen added, "We think about resiliency holistically... it's clearly a financial resiliency culture within HCA."
  • Benjamin Mayo, Leerink Partners: Queried on 2026 capital deployment. Hazen replied, "It's a reasonable assumption to assume that our plan is going to be somewhat consistent with the methodologies we've used in the past."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally neutral, focusing on the specifics of supplemental payments, market demand, and expense management, with some probing on guidance conservatism and potential volume impacts from policy changes.
  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone, especially on federal policy uncertainties, often noting the "fluid nature of the federal policy environment" and stating, "We believe our resiliency plan should provide some relief."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a similarly measured and pragmatic tone, though management expressed greater confidence in operational execution this quarter, citing "disciplined operations" and "enhanced capabilities."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenues, net income, adjusted EBITDA, and EPS was raised compared to last quarter's outlook, reflecting improved operational performance and increased supplemental payment benefits.
  • The company reported stronger volume growth and better payer mix in Q3, as well as a larger benefit from Medicaid supplemental programs.
  • Management highlighted more robust execution in expense management and resiliency efforts this quarter. Analysts continued to focus on supplemental payments, policy uncertainties, and volume trends, mirroring last quarter's concerns.
  • Management's tone showed increased confidence in sustaining high levels of performance, while analysts remained focused on the durability of growth and the impact of federal policy changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hazen and Marks identified policy uncertainty regarding the extension of enhanced premium tax credits for exchanges as a significant risk, noting, "At this point, however, we still do not know how this policy will play out."
  • The complex and variable nature of Medicaid supplemental payment programs was highlighted, with Marks stating, "These programs are complex, variable in timing and do not fully cover our cost to treat Medicaid patients."
  • Analysts questioned the impact of pending state approvals, potential exchange disruptions, and the durability of volume growth should policy changes not materialize as hoped.
  • Management emphasized ongoing resiliency planning and operational discipline as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

HCA Healthcare management conveyed that the company delivered robust third-quarter results with substantial year-over-year growth in earnings and revenue, driven by strong demand, improved payer mix, and higher Medicaid supplemental payments. With full-year guidance raised across key metrics and a continued focus on disciplined execution, operational resiliency, and capital investment, management expressed confidence in sustaining performance while closely monitoring policy developments that could affect demand and reimbursement in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ontario will pause Reagan ad after Trump's outburst, Doug Ford says
data.description:
Doug Ford, the premier of Ontario, Canada, said Friday the east-central province will pause an ad featuring former U.S. President Ronald Reagan's criticism of tariffs, aiming to reopen trade talks with the U.S.

The ad prompted President Donald Trump on Thursday to end all trade negotiations with Canada, accusing the country of trying to influence a pending U.S. Supreme Court case that could doom many of his tariffs.

Prior to his decision to suspend the ad, effective Monday, Ford said he had talked to Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney.

“Our intention was always to initiate a conversation about the kind of economy that Americans want to build and the impact of tariffs on workers and businesses,” Ford wrote in a post on X, referencing the ad.

“We’ve achieved our goal, having reached U.S. audiences at the highest levels,” He added. “I’ve directed my team to keep putting our message in front of Americans over the weekend so that we can air our commercial during the first two World Series games.”

Related tickers: (EWC), (BBCA), (FLCA).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Alliant hiked to Buy at UBS on data center power opportunities
data.description:
Alliant Energy (NASDAQ:LNT) +0.7% in Friday's trading as UBS upgraded shares to Buy from Neutral with a $79 price target, bumped up from $74, citing the utility company's unique position to capitalize on additional load and generation investment required to meet surging data center demand.

UBS analysts led by William Apicelli raised their FY 2028 earnings estimate to $4.13/share vs. $3.97 consensus, saying the higher estimate is supported by expectations for the QTS-Madison data center, and the bank anticipates this capital will be incorporated into plans on the anticipated Q3 call on November 7.

Apicelli believes Alliant's (LNT) current valuation reflects minimal value for data center growth, considering the magnitude of the capex relative to the company's size, which it sees as a significant growth opportunity.

The bank expects continued significant and sustained interest from data centers seeking power
solutions, noting neighboring utilities have announced data center projects upwards of 2 GW, showing the surging demand in its Wisconsin-Iowa region, and Alliant (LNT) has indicated a requirement for 1.5 GW of new generation capacity to support load demands in its discussions with data centers.

data.title: Comfort Systems USA signals continued high-teen same-store revenue growth through 2025 while backlog reaches record $9.4B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Comfort Systems USA (FIX) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Lane opened by highlighting that "We earned $8.25 per share this quarter, which is double what we earned in the same quarter last year." Lane pointed to a sharp increase in mechanical business profitability, higher electrical segment results, and favorable late-stage project developments. He also noted, "Our backlog at the end of the quarter grew to a new high of $9.4 billion," and that the company achieved a second consecutive same-store backlog increase of more than $1 billion. Lane announced the acquisition of FZ Electrical and Meisner Electric effective October 1 and a 20% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.60 per share.
  • CFO William George stated, "Our third quarter results were remarkable in every way with 33% same-store revenue growth, sharply higher margins, EPS up by over 100% from the prior year and a surge of over $500 million in quarterly free cash flow." George reported revenue of $2.5 billion, a $639 million increase over the prior year, and highlighted that "Electric segment revenue grew by 71% and mechanical revenue increased by 26%." He added, "We achieved more than $400 million in quarterly EBITDA for the first time ever, and that's a 74% increase over the same quarter 1 year ago."
  • COO Trent McKenna underscored, "Our backlog at the end of the third quarter was a record $9.4 billion, a large sequential and large year-over-year increase...same-store backlog 62% higher than at this time last year." McKenna also noted, "Industrial customers accounted for 65% of total revenue...Technology, which is included in Industrial, was 42% of our revenue, a substantial increase from 32% in the prior year."

Outlook

  • George provided guidance that "our best estimate is that fourth quarter same-store revenue will grow in the high-teen range as compared to the same quarter last year. For full year 2026, we expect same-store revenue growth to continue most likely by a percentage in the low to mid-teens and weighed more heavily to the first half of the year."
  • Management indicated, "We currently expect that 2026 profit margins are likely to continue in the strong ranges that we have achieved and averaged over recent quarters."
  • Lane said, "With solid bookings and great demand, we expect continuing growth and strong results in 2025 and 2026."

Financial Results

  • Comfort Systems reported revenue of $2.5 billion for the third quarter of 2025, gross profit of $608 million, and a gross profit percentage of 24.8%. Operating income for the quarter was $379 million. Net income was $292 million, or $8.25 per share. Quarterly EBITDA reached $414 million, and free cash flow for the quarter was $519 million.
  • SG&A expense was $230 million, or 9.4% of revenue. The company ended September with a net cash position of $725 million.
  • George highlighted, "We acquired 2 fantastic companies on October 1...expected to provide over $200 million in incremental annual revenue and $15 million to $20 million of annual EBITDA."

Q&A

  • Adam Thalhimer, Thompson, Davis & Company: Asked about technology sector bidding activity and capital allocation. Lane responded that "the opportunities, the pipeline is still robust, matching quarter 3. There's still more opportunities than probably can be handled out there in the market at the moment." George explained capital allocation priorities, emphasizing, "We will deploy most of our cash doing acquisitions. We will continually buy back our shares...and we get aggressive on that when we feel like the stock has dipped."
  • Sangita Jain, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Asked about the sustainability of free cash flow and backlog timing. George answered, "We are a cash business. We pay our taxes in cash. So when you see a quarter where we have cash flow above our net income, at some point, we're going to give that back."
  • Brent Thielman, D.A. Davidson & Co.: Asked about labor constraints and modular capacity. Lane stated, "We're constantly recruiting, but we do get people to come here and work...We're improving productivity constantly."
  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair: Inquired about backlog starting timeline and service revenue. George clarified, "The majority of the backlog numerically is jobs that have already started...all the new bookings are going to start sometime within the next year." McKenna described service as "a day-to-day kind of bunt single doubles business...we're just seeing broad strength in that business, and it's execution driven."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of growth, backlog quality, and capital allocation, with a positive to slightly cautious tone as they pressed for clarity on cash flow sustainability, labor constraints, and backlog timing.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, with Lane expressing optimism about demand and growth, and George emphasizing strong margins and cash flow. Responses were direct, with occasional elaboration on longer-term strategy but no notable defensiveness.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a positive sentiment, though analysts probed more deeply on sustainability and scalability issues given the heightened growth figures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for same-store revenue growth shifted higher for Q4 2025, now described as "high-teen range," compared to "mid-teen" for the full year in Q2. Backlog hit a new record of $9.4 billion, up from $8.1 billion in Q2. Technology as a percentage of revenue increased to 42% from 40% last quarter.
  • Management's tone remained confident, with added emphasis on the magnitude and quality of backlog and continued discipline in capital allocation. Analysts' focus shifted more toward sustainability and execution risks as growth rates accelerated.
  • The strategic focus remained on disciplined project selection, modular expansion, and targeted acquisitions, with a more explicit discussion of labor force management and automation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited no extraordinary events impacting cash flow this quarter and indicated that strong cash flow is expected to align with net income going forward.
  • Questions from analysts centered on labor constraints, sustainability of backlog growth, and the ability to maintain hiring and productivity levels.
  • Management explained ongoing investments in recruiting, apprenticeship programs, and automation in modular, and reiterated a preference for project and customer selectivity to manage risk.

Final Takeaway

Comfort Systems USA management emphasized record results driven by strong demand in technology and industrial sectors, record backlog, and disciplined growth through acquisitions and internal investments. The company projects continued high-teen same-store revenue growth into late 2025, with robust margins and cash flow supporting further expansion and shareholder returns. Management's outlook remains optimistic as it continues to prioritize operational execution, workforce development, and strategic capital deployment to sustain momentum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Booz Allen cut to Sell equivalent at BofA as National Security can't offset Civil weakness
data.description:
Booz Allen Hamilton (NYSE:BAH) -10.2% in Friday's trading to a more than two-year low $88.11 after narrowly missing Q2 adjusted earnings estimates and cutting full-year earnings guidance below Wall Street consensus, as slower civilian spending and timing shifts hit the company's results and outlook.

The company lowered guidance for FY 2026 adjusted EPS to $5.45-$5.65 from $6.20-$6.55 and below $6.30 analyst consensus estimate, revenues of $11.3 billion-$11.5 billion from $12 billion-$12.5 billion and compared to $12.06 billion consensus, and full-year adjusted EBITDA to $1.19 billion-$1.22 billion from $1.32 billion-$1.37 billion.

Bank of America double downgraded Booz Allen (BAH)‌ to Underperform from Buy with a $90 price target, slashed from $160, citing the magnitude of the decline in the company's Civil segment, with potential for more downside to come, while the strong position across the National Security portfolio is not enough to offset growing pressure from Civil or propel growth in the near term.

BofA previously had seen Booz Allen's 7% headcount reduction and plan to "reset and restructure" the Civil business as a "clearing the decks" event, but the continued turmoil in civilian agency priorities, lack of a September budget flush and continued uncertainty around the budget funding including the government shutdown pose further risks to the downside.

On top of declines seen throughout H1, BofA analysts now expect an additional 30% decline in Civil revenues for H2 2026 vs. H1 2026 compared to their prior outlook for a 5% drop.

J.P. Morgan analyst Seth Seifman, who rates the stock at Underweight, said the market understood the potential for a guidance cut, but the cut presented by Booz Allen (BAH) is "steep," as the presidential transition has "generated more challenges for BAH than for most other services contractors, particularly on the civil side."

William Blair analyst Louie DiPalma, who rates the stock at Outperform, said he expects the guidance cut will "serve as a clearing event, given the strong bookings," and believes Booz Allen (BAH) will experience a rebound in FY 2027.
